notes: Explicit NPR/NPB/NPG formulation rows and variant inheritance survive into completed Stage2; numeric detail is concentrated in selected rows rather than uniformly row-complete.
Nanosphere and nanocapsule formulations are explicit and grounded; varying concentration series is visible, but the relation between all study variables and every row is only partially marked.
DOE factors, table anchors, and governed DOE scope are present; the April 6 DOE slice shows legal trigger sufficiency under the existing DOE unit.

Eight explicit polymer and drug-loaded formulations are usable, but weak DOE-like study language never becomes governed DOE scope; April 6 diagnostics classify this as a correct scope-layer exclusion.
Cryoprotectant variants are identified and table-anchored, but most variant identity remains label-bound in the completed Stage2 projection.
Nanosphere and nanorod process-condition variants are enumerated well enough for downstream use; some provenance is table-heavy rather than evenly split across text and tables.
The study mixes formulation and property-comparison framing, but the three designed nanoparticle variants are explicit and well distinguished.
Stage2 names Formulation 1-12 and detects surfactant and PEG factors, but it does not bind those factors to the numbered variants strongly enough for reliable downstream reconstruction.
Drug:polymer ratio variants are captured and partly reflected in identity metadata, though some stored and variant rows remain sparse in projected structured fields.
Stage2 captures optimization factor ranges and an optimal formulation, but it does not construct an enumerable formulation universe from the factor sweep under any existing unit.
Single AP-PLGA-NP and control formulations are explicit; abstract-level factor language is correctly not promoted into DOE-style expansion.
Box-Behnken factors, table anchors, and governed DOE scope are sufficient; the April 6 DOE slice confirms successful existing-unit execution with 26 emitted rows.
Method and SC6OH-amount variants are explicit and strongly anchored, although some numeric values remain text-normalized rather than fully basis-normalized.
The paper centers on one main formulation plus comparator variants, so limited variable discovery is acceptable; the Stage2 output is usable but comparatively sparse.
Full-factorial formulations are already enumerated row-wise with factor values and measurements, so no additional function-unit expansion is needed.
